english: Frog legs fried with fish sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried frog with fish sauce is approximately 300 g, typically served as a shared main dish or individual entrée in a Vietnamese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Frog legs, raw (FDC ID 174474) combined with fish sauce nutrient profile (FDC ID 173580) and frying oil adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese fried frog with fish sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw frog legs (FDC ID 174474), fish sauce (FDC ID 173580), and a standard deep-frying oil absorption factor (~8–10% fat uptake per 100g cooked weight). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA-derived composite and retained where consistent. Energy recalculated as: protein 15.0g × 4 + fat 11.67g × 9 + carbohydrate 5.0g × 4 = 60 + 105.03 + 20 = 185 kcal ≈ 183 kcal (input value accepted within rounding tolerance). Sodium 600 mg/100g is consistent with fish sauce contribution at typical recipe proportions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on battered/coated fried meat and fish sauce glaze
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese fried frog with fish sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous fried protein dishes with minimal carbohydrate coating (cornstarch-battered fried meat/fish, GI range 30–45 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar preparations). Carbohydrate content is low (5g/100g), primarily from cornstarch coating and sugar in glaze. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (300g × 5g/100g = 15g) / 100 = 5.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100g from fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 300g serving delivers 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg, and fish sauce should be replaced with low-sodium seasoning or the portion reduced to no more than 50g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5g/100g) but high fat (11.67g/100g) and high sodium (600mg/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5g/100g) and high protein (15g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but the high fat from deep-frying (11.67g/100g) and elevated sodium (600mg/100g) warrant portion control; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request pan-fried or grilled preparation and limit portion to 150g or less.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (600 mg/100g), total fat (11.67g/100g), saturated fat (2.67g/100g), and trans fat (0.17g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (600mg/100g), total fat from deep-frying, saturated fat (2.67g/100g), and trans fat (0.17g/100g) poses a significant cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations and request grilled frog legs with minimal fish sauce, limiting intake to occasional small portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; sodium 600mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; chilli is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its deep-fried form due to AGE generation, high sodium (600mg/100g) worsening tissue swelling, and chilli as a nightshade trigger; grilled frog legs with reduced fish sauce are a safer alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Frog with Fish Sauce (Vietnamese: Ếch Chiên Nước Mắm) is a Vietnamese dish in which frog legs are marinated, coated in a light cornstarch batter, deep-fried or pan-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a savoury-sweet glaze made from fish sauce, garlic, chilli, sugar, and lime juice, or served with a fish sauce dipping condiment on the side. The dish is popular in Vietnam and Vietnamese restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The frog meat is tender and mild-flavoured, similar in texture to chicken, with a crispy exterior from frying. The fish sauce glaze imparts a pungent, umami-rich, slightly sweet and salty coating. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 183 kcal, 15g protein, 11.67g fat, and 5g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 600mg driven by fish sauce. The high sodium makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The low carbohydrate content and high protein make it moderately suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled and prepared with reduced fat. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory conditions. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is typically served as a shared main dish or individual entrée in a Vietnamese meal context.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this dish exists in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw frog legs and fish sauce with a standard frying oil absorption adjustment. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA-derived composite and found to be internally consistent. Confidence is therefore medium rather than low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central: frog legs raw (FDC ID 174474) and fish sauce (FDC ID 173580) with deep-frying fat absorption adjustment (~8–10% fat uptake). No exact approved-source entry exists for this prepared Vietnamese dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this prepared Vietnamese fried frog dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source at the level of a composite prepared dish as of 2026. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 35, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar fried protein dishes with minimal carbohydrate coating; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI database. 4. Halal status is marked false as frog is considered haram in mainstream Hanafi Islamic jurisprudence; consumers should verify with their religious authority. 5. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, reflecting the GrabFood listing context; region_of_origin is set to Vietnam as the dish's culinary origin. 6. trans_fat_g 0.17g/100g is retained from input; this is plausible for deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated oils at commercial scale.
